pattern: Because the array is sorted, jumping straight between two far-apart indices never beats walking the adjacent steps between them one at a time -- so the only real decision is which adjacent steps get the flat discount for being someone's closest neighbor, which turns every query into a prefix-sum range lookup.
hints: A direct jump from x to y costs exactly the sum of the ordinary adjacent-step costs along the way (the array is sorted, so the differences telescope), so direct jumps never help -- only the discounted closest-neighbor move can ever be cheaper than the plain adjacent step.

For every adjacent pair (i, i+1), work out independently in each direction whether i+1 is i's closest neighbor (discount that direction to cost 1) and whether i is (i+1)'s closest neighbor (discount that direction to cost 1) -- the two directions can have different costs.
Build prefix sums of the left-to-right step costs and the right-to-left step costs once; each query then just subtracts two prefix values depending on whether it moves forward or backward.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, three points and three queries
statement example, four points and three queries
query from an index to itself costs nothing
a tie in adjacent distances resolves to the smaller index
one huge gap next to several tiny gaps
evenly spaced values, every step equally close both ways

stress.description: random strictly increasing arrays with random queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 5000))
    nums = sorted(rng.sample(range(-1000000000, 1000000000), n))
    q = min(2000, size)
    queries = [[rng.randint(0, n - 1), rng.randint(0, n - 1)] for _ in range(q)]
    return [nums, queries]
